- b4 and b5 must be at the same location.
- b1 (and thus b2,b3) must not be at the same location as b4 or b5. Since b4 and b5 are together, this means the location of {b1,b2,b3} != location of {b4,b5}.
- Also b4 and b5 must be together.

So we need to move blocks to satisfy these constraints. We want the shortest plan (lowest number of actions). We can choose any target coordinates within bounds, as long as the constraints hold.

We need to find a plan that moves blocks to some meeting points.

Let's denote target for group A (b1,b2,b3) as (Xa, Ya). Target for group B (b4,b5) as (Xb, Yb). Must have (Xa,Ya) != (Xb,Yb).

We need to minimize total number of moves (Manhattan distance sum from initial to target, since each action moves one block by 1). Since we can choose any target, we should choose targets that minimize total moves.

But also we need to ensure that the moves are feasible (no collisions? The domain doesn't forbid two blocks occupying same cell; there is no collision precondition. So blocks can occupy same cell at any time. So we just need to pick targets and move each block along a shortest path (any order) to its target.

Thus the problem reduces to: choose two distinct cells (Xa,Ya) and (Xb,Yb) to minimize sum of Manhattan distances for each group.

Group A: b1 (11,12), b2 (12,5), b3 (10,18).
Group B: b4 (2,9), b5 (3,1).

We need to pick (Xa,Ya) and (Xb,Yb) distinct.

Total cost = |11-Xa|+|12-Ya| + |12-Xa|+|5-Ya| + |10-Xa|+|18-Ya| + |2-Xb|+|9-Yb| + |3-Xb|+|1-Yb|.

We can minimize each group independently, except the two target cells must be distinct.

For group A, the optimal meeting point (minimizing sum of Manhattan distances) is any median of the x-coordinates and any median of the y-coordinates. For x: {11,12,10} median is 11.
